segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, digital-themed title sequence displaying 'November 5, 2019' and the 'FRONTLINE' logo in bold white letters on a red backdrop, accompanied by suspenseful, orchestral music. A dimly lit server room with rows of black server racks illuminated by red indicator lights appears as a male voiceover declares, "Tonight. The race to become an AI superpower is on." This transitions to a close-up of a man in a suit with a serious expression, while the voiceover continues, "The politics of artificial intelligence." A futuristic interface then shows facial recognition data with Chinese text overlaying people in a crowd, as an expert male speaker states, "There'll be a Chinese tech sector, and there'll be an American tech sector." The scene shifts to a formal assembly where a man in a suit is applauded by others in a large hall, with the expert male speaker referring to "The new tech war." A yellow robotic arm in a factory is then shown, as the expert male speaker explains, "The more data, the better the AI works." A semi-truck labeled 'Betsy Mae' with a blue ribbon follows, while the expert male speaker asserts, "So, in the age of AI, where data's the new oil, China is the new Saudi Arabia." A warehouse filled with autonomous 'Ocado' robots moves in synchronized patterns, as the expert male speaker introduces "The future of work." The expert male speaker then appears directly addressing the camera, stating, "When I increase productivity through automation, jobs go away. I believe about 50% of jobs will be somewhat or extremely threatened by AI in the next 15 years or so." A close-up of a Facebook interface prompting 'Write about yourself' appears, as the expert male speaker introduces "AI and corporate surveillance," followed by a female expert stating, "We thought that we were searching Google. We had no idea that Google was searching us." The sequence ends with an aerial view of a Google building with solar panels, and a final shot of a surveillance camera mounted on a pole in a dimly lit urban setting, as the female expert concludes her point and the expert male speaker introduces "And the threat to democracy."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a nighttime urban scene featuring a large digital display showing surveillance footage with Chinese text identifying individuals as '涉嫌违法人员' (suspected lawbreakers) and age/gender labels, visually illustrating a public facial recognition system that, as a male voice states, "is on its way to building a total surveillance state." This transitions to a close-up of a white LG CLOi robot with glowing blue eyes, suggesting AI integration in daily life, as a female voice announces, "Tonight on Frontline," followed by another female voice saying "Hello," and then a third female voice commenting on how AI "has pervaded so many elements of everyday life," questioning, "How do we make it transparent and accountable?" A hand then holds a smartphone displaying a live feed with a facial recognition interface, zooming into a woman identified as 'KATE LAUREN' with 'SIMILARITY 98.3%', all under the overarching theme introduced by a male voice: "In the Age of AI." A sequence of funding credits for FRONTLINE, including logos for the MacArthur Foundation, Ford Foundation, and others, with text acknowledging PBS viewers and major supporters, plays out accompanied by dramatic, suspenseful orchestral music featuring a prominent gong sound. The scene shifts to a dimly lit room where two men are playing Go, with close-ups of hands placing black and white stones on the board, as the orchestral music fades and a resonant string pluck introduces a male voice explaining, "This is the world's most complex board game. There are more possible moves in the game of Go than there are atoms in the universe. Legend has it that in 2300 BCE..." Text overlays credit the writers and producers, David Fanning and Neil Docherty, and the directors, Neil Docherty and David Fanning. The final frames show a close-up of a man in a suit, focused and contemplative, as he plays Go, emphasizing the strategic depth of the game and possibly drawing a parallel to AI decision-making, while the male voice continues to narrate the game's ancient origins.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an intense close-up of a man’s eyes, conveying deep concentration, followed by hands placing black and white Go stones on a board, as a deep, resonant male narrator's voice explains that "Emperor Yao devised it to teach his son discipline, concentration, and balance." Traditional, mysterious background music accompanies this historical context. The narrator continues, stating that "over 4,000 years later, this ancient Chinese game would signal the start of a new industrial age," as the scene transitions to a dark screen filled with complex mathematical equations. The music shifts to a more modern, electronic tone as the title 'IN THE AGE OF AI' resolves in bold, illuminated text. The narrative then shifts to a formal setting in Seoul, South Korea, in 2016, where the man in a suit is greeted by staff and surrounded by media, suggesting a high-profile event. He is later seen shaking hands on stage at a 'Challenge Match' event dated 8–15 March 2016, with a backdrop featuring network diagrams. A female reporter's voice describes this as a "breakthrough moment when the world champion of the ancient board game Go takes on an AI program developed by Google." The man in the suit speaks into a microphone in Korean, stating his belief that "human intuition is still too advanced for AI to have caught up." The scene cuts to a crowded audience, some using laptops, and then to a large outdoor screen in a city street displaying the Go game, indicating public interest. Another English male voice discusses the game's popularity in countries like China, Japan, and South Korea, as the video ends with a close-up of a Go board and the man in the suit, reinforcing the theme of human intellect versus artificial intelligence, with the music fading out.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Pedro Domingos from the University of Washington, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting with the FRONTLINE logo, explaining that for Go players, it's more than a game—it's a spiritual component for strategy. He highlights Lee Se-dol as the world's greatest Go player and a national hero in South Korea, noting the widespread belief that Lee Se-dol would easily beat AlphaGo. The scene then shifts to a high-energy media event, where Lee Se-dol, in a suit, is mobbed by photographers and journalists, capturing his arrival with cameras and smartphones amidst a charged atmosphere of anticipation and camera clicks. The setting transitions to a formal Go match venue, where a large screen counts down to the 'Google DeepMind Challenge Match' (8–15 March 2016). A narrator explains that Google's AlphaGo was a self-teaching computer program, developed from Go rules and historical games. The match commences with Lee Se-dol intensely focused across from AlphaGo, monitors displaying the game, while the AlphaGo team observes. A commentator recalls the games were watched by tens of millions, with background Korean speech audible. Public reactions show crowds in a store watching on multiple TVs, and a Korean news ticker announces Lee Se-dol’s defeat. The narrator emphasizes the event's significance as a sports spectacle with national pride at stake across Southeast Asia, and the public unveiling of deep learning AI. The video concludes with a man on stage presenting the match's progress, a large screen displaying a game graph, underscoring the global technological stakes, all accompanied by continuous background music.

The scene quickly shifts to a high-energy media event where a man in a suit—later identified as Lee Se-dol—is surrounded by a throng of photographers and journalists, capturing his arrival with cameras and smartphones. He walks through the crowd, waving, as the atmosphere is charged with anticipation. The setting transitions to a formal Go match venue, where a large screen counts down to the start of the 'Google DeepMind Challenge Match' (8–15 March 2016). The match begins with Lee Se-dol seated across from AlphaGo, with monitors displaying the game board and timers. The camera captures close-ups of Lee Se-dol’s intense focus as he makes his first move, while the AlphaGo team observes from behind a blue partition. The scene cuts to public reactions: crowds gather in a store, watching the match on multiple TV screens, and a news ticker in Korean announces Lee Se-dol’s defeat. The video concludes with a presentation by a man in a suit on stage, explaining the match’s progress to an audience, with a large screen displaying a graph of the game’s development, emphasizing the global significance and technological stakes of the event.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a tablet and laptop displaying a Go game, with a hand interacting, before transitioning to Nicholas Thompson, Editor-in-Chief of Wired. Speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting, Thompson articulates in a clear, thoughtful voice how machine learning and AI, specifically AlphaGo, are fed Go games, study them, learn, and generate moves "humans hadn't thought of before." This explanation is visually reinforced by a dynamic sequence of multiple Go boards in a grid, symbolizing AI's analytical complexity. The narrative then shifts to the Google DeepMind Challenge Match, showing Lee Se-dol, the South Korean Go grandmaster, deeply focused at a wooden Go board, wearing a dark suit, with a monitor displaying the game. A broadcast screen shows match details and timers. During this, a commentator exclaims in Korean, then in English, "Oh, totally unthinkable move," with another voice adding, "I thought it was a mistake." A distinct voiceover announces, "Game two. Move 37." Lee Se-dol’s reactions, from intense concentration to contemplative gestures, convey the match's psychological weight. A wide shot reveals the AlphaGo team observing from behind a blue partition. The segment concludes with AI scientist Kai-Fu Lee, speaking calmly and analytically in an interview setting, providing expert commentary on "move 37," describing it as a "brilliant" move that humans "could not fathom."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of South Korean Go grandmaster Lee Se-dol, intensely focused as he places a black stone on the Go board. A male voice, identified as Kai-Fu Lee, remarks, "Wow, after thousands years of playing, we never thought about making a move like that." This is immediately followed by another male voice announcing, "Oh, he resigned. It looks like Lee Se-dol has just resigned actually." The scene transitions to a wider shot of the formal event stage, where two men in suits stand beside a large Go board display, clapping and celebrating, with the backdrop reading 'Google DeepMind Challenge Match 8–15 March 2016'. A narrator explains that "In the end, the scientists watched their algorithms win four of the games. Lee Se-dol took one." The camera then cuts to the AlphaGo team in a control room, where team members in black shirts with 'AlphaGo TEAM' printed on the back are gathered around multiple monitors displaying real-time data and the live game feed, visibly excited, clapping and smiling. Kai-Fu Lee's voiceover continues, stating, "What happened with Go first and foremost was a huge victory for DeepMind and for AI... It wasn't that the computers beat the humans, it was that, you know, one type of intelligence beat another." The video then cuts to close-ups of key figures: a man with gray hair in a dark suit (Pedro Domingos) speaking thoughtfully, while the narrator's voice explains that "Artificial intelligence had proven it could marshal a vast amount of data beyond anything any human could handle and use it to teach itself how to predict an outcome. The commercial implications were enormous." This narration continues over Kai-Fu Lee, an AI scientist in a blue suit and patterned tie, speaking with a calm, analytical demeanor. The control room is shown again, with detailed views of the monitors displaying performance metrics and game analysis, emphasizing the technical depth of the AI’s operation. Kai-Fu Lee's voice returns, reflecting, "While AlphaGo is a is a toy game, but its success and its um uh waking everyone up, I think is is going to be remembered as..." as the sequence ends with him continuing his interview, reinforcing the significance of the match.

One monitor shows a tablet with the match timer and a small video feed of the players. The team members are visibly excited, clapping and smiling. The sequence ends with Kai-Fu Lee continuing his interview, reinforcing the significance of the match.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Kai-Fu Lee, an AI scientist in a dark blue suit, light blue shirt, and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit, professional setting with blurred bookshelves, describing "the pivotal moment where AI became mature and everybody jumped on the bandwagon." Dramatic, suspenseful background music then begins as the scene transitions to a bustling media event where a man in a suit is surrounded by photographers and journalists, capturing the moment, while a male narrator states, "This is about the consequences of that defeat." A futuristic digital interface then shows a man walking through a forest with a digital overlay scanning his face, suggesting advanced surveillance, as the narrator continues, "How the AI algorithms are ushering in a new age of great potential and prosperity." This cuts to a large public screen displaying a woman’s face with a facial recognition interface, showing Chinese text and age/gender labels, as the narrator warns of an age that "will also deepen inequality, challenge democracy, and divide the world into two AI superpowers." An outdoor urban scene follows, with a person riding a scooter down Clinton Avenue under bright sunlight, casting long shadows, as the narrator announces, "Tonight, five stories about how artificial intelligence is changing our world," accompanied by swelling dramatic music. The narrative then moves to a formal assembly where a man in a dark suit stands before a crowd, receiving applause in a grand hall with red seating and Chinese banners, hinting at a political or state event, still set to the ongoing dramatic music. This is followed by a nighttime shot of the U.S. Capitol building, illuminated against a dark sky with an American flag waving, symbolizing governmental authority. The video then transitions to a dark, abstract digital collage of fragmented images and data, culminating in a title card reading 'ONE CHINA HAS A PLAN' over a night-time cityscape with illuminated skyscrapers, including the China World Tower, as the narrator states, "China has decided to chase the AI future." The final scenes show a high-tech event, likely Baidu World 2018, with attendees walking through a corridor lined with glowing 'Baidu' logos, followed by a man in a white shirt and dark pants giving a presentation on a stage with dynamic blue light patterns, engaging an audience in a futuristic, tech-focused environment, while a voice (likely Kai-Fu Lee) discusses "the difference between the internet mindset and the AI mindset."

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins in a dimly lit, futuristic environment bathed in blue light, showing a crowd and a man before a glowing interface displaying an 'EMERGENCY WARNING' in Chinese and English, as a narrator declares, "A future made and embraced by a new generation." This transitions to a woman in a white shirt using a smartphone with a digital overlay for '设备指纹' and '安全验证', highlighting AI-driven identity verification. The scene then shifts to a modern tech event, where Orville Schell, an older man with white hair and glasses in a suit and patterned tie, interacts with attendees and observes displays, including one labeled 'User Study with an Overhead View'. Schell, speaking with a distinct American accent, reflects on the "immense energy" and "very younger people" generating this new technology. As he recounts, "When I first came here in 1975, Chairman Mao was still alive, the Cultural Revolution was coming on," the visuals intercut with colorized vintage archival footage from 1970s-1980s China, depicting daily life in Tiananmen Square, a snowy street, children on a stone pillar, and students in a classroom. Schell emphasizes the stark contrast, stating, "there wasn't a single whiff of anything of what you see here. It was unimaginable," creating a powerful audio-visual comparison between past and present, all underscored by a light, futuristic background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of an older Man in Suit (Serious Expression) with white hair and glasses, wearing a gray suit, patterned tie, and a lanyard, speaking thoughtfully in a dimly lit, bustling tech event with blue ambient lighting, stating, "And the fact that it has gone through so many different changes since, is quite extraordinary." Faint background chatter is heard. A deep, authoritative narrator's voice then declares, "This extraordinary progress goes back to that game of Go," as the scene transitions to a futuristic, high-tech presentation where a man walks down a glowing corridor with 'YES, I DO' projected on the ground, followed by a close-up of a hand holding a robotic hand, symbolizing advanced AI integration. The next sequence shows a man standing before a large digital screen displaying a risk assessment interface with Chinese text like '审批不通过' (approval denied) and 'WARNING', indicating a failed check, while a woman in a white shirt observes. Paul Mozur from The New York Times, a man with a beard in a dark blue shirt, then speaks from an office environment with computer monitors, explaining, "I think that the government recognized that this was a sort of critical thing for the future and we need to catch up in this, that you know, we cannot have a foreign company showing us up at our own game, and this is going to be something that is going to be critically important in the future. Um, so you know, we called it the Sputnik moment for for the Chinese government. The Chinese government kind of woke up." The video then cuts to a large formal assembly, the Belt and Road Forum for International Cooperation, where a Man in a dark suit and red tie speaks at a podium against a solid blue backdrop, stating in Chinese, translated as, "As we often say in China, the beginning is the most difficult part." Wide shots reveal the massive, diverse audience in suits, attentively listening at long tables in the grand hall with wooden paneling and bright overhead lighting, beneath a banner clearly reading 'Belt and Road Forum for International Cooperation'. The authoritative narrator's voice continues, providing context: "In 2017, Xi Jinping announced the government's bold new plans to an audience of foreign diplomats. China would catch up with the US in artificial intelligence by 2025 and lead the world by 2030," followed by another speaker mentioning "intensified cooperation in frontier areas such as digital economy."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the Belt and Road Forum for International Cooperation, showcasing a grand hall filled with thousands of attendees under bright overhead lighting and a large banner. A dramatic, slightly futuristic orchestral score begins as a clear male voice introduces key technological advancements: "Artificial intelligence, nanotechnology, and quantum computing." The scene transitions to a high-tech warehouse, where red autonomous robots, labeled with numbers like '6350' and branded likely as JD Logistics, move swiftly across a grid of yellow docking stations in an organized space marked with Chinese signs for 'Intelligence' and 'Success through the Right Path'. The narrator states, "Today, China leads the world in e-commerce," and mentions "Drones deliver to rural villages." He continues, describing a society that "bypassed credit cards" and "now shops in stores without cashiers, where the currency is facial recognition." This narration accompanies visuals of an outdoor surveillance camera capturing a red box, a futuristic interface displaying a man’s face within a digital overlay, and a close-up of a smartphone screen scanning a woman’s face with Chinese prompts '请正对摄像头' (Please face the camera) and '识别中...' (Identifying...). The sequence continues at a 7FRESH Food Market, where a customer scans a product, and a smartphone displays a ¥81.8 payment followed by a successful facial recognition confirmation. In a modern office hallway, a large screen powered by Face++ technology displays employee faces and names for biometric access. A second male voice then emphasizes, "No country has ever moved that fast," noting that in "a short two and a half years, uh China's AI implementation really went from minimal amount to probably about 17 or 18 unicorns, that is billion-dollar companies in AI today. And that that progress is um is hard to believe." The video concludes with a man in a suit speaking in a dimly lit tech environment with a blurred audience, as the first narrator's voice returns, stating, "The progress was powered by a new generation of ambitious young techs pouring out of..." before fading.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a bright, active modern open-plan office, branded with 'LEARNING' and 'LIFE', where employees work amidst computer monitors. A male narrator, with a deep voice, describes Chinese universities competing for new ideas, financed by Chinese venture capitalists, introducing this as Sinovation, created by US-educated AI scientist and businessman Kai-Fu Lee. Kai-Fu Lee, in a dark suit and tie, walks through the office, approaching a large display wall featuring 'Autonomous Driving & Robots' and 'Artificial Intelligence' logos like 'OURS' and 'Ufactory'. He points to the display and speaks directly to the camera, his clear, articulate voice explaining that they have "six unicorns" in the AI area, defining a unicorn as a billion-dollar company, and mentioning they show two unicorns representing five billion dollars or higher. The scene briefly cuts to a digital screen displaying a Face++ biometric system with Kai-Fu Lee's photo labeled '李开复 员工', before returning to him walking through the office. The narrator then provides biographical details, stating Kai-Fu Lee was born in Taiwan, sent to high school in Tennessee, and his PhD thesis at Carnegie Mellon on computer speech recognition led him to Apple. The segment concludes with vintage archival footage of a woman in a lavender blazer smiling, followed by an old Apple computer screen displaying 'Good Morning America! And good morning, Joan.', from which a slightly tinny female voice is heard discussing Apple Computer's new developments.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with vintage archival footage from the 1980s, showing a classic Apple computer screen displaying 'Good Morning America! And good morning, Joan.' in a serif font, while a woman in a lavender blazer and black turtleneck smiles warmly beside it. A narrator introduces Kai-Fu Lee as the inventor of Apple speech recognition technology, followed by a computerized voice demonstrating this technology, stating, "Casper, copy this to make right too. Casper, paste. Casper, 72 point italic outline." The narrator continues, detailing Kai-Fu Lee's career progression to Microsoft Research in Asia and head of Google China, before founding Sinovation in Beijing 10 years ago to find promising startups and AI talent. The scene transitions to a modern office where Kai-Fu Lee, in a dark suit, light purple shirt, and patterned tie, walks through an open-plan workspace filled with employees at desks, some with dual monitors displaying code. He stops to speak with a young man in a black hoodie labeled 'AI Empowering Business' and another in a green jacket, gesturing as he explains something. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Kai-Fu Lee speaking directly to the camera, his expression serious and thoughtful, as he explains that Chinese entrepreneurial companies started as copycats but have developed their own form of entrepreneurship over the last 15 years, which he describes as "tenacious, very fast, winner take all, and incredible work ethic." He adds that "these a few thousand Chinese top entrepreneurs, they could take on any entrepreneur anywhere in the world." As he speaks, a dramatic, inspiring orchestral background music begins. The scene shifts to a busy urban street with multiple vehicles, including a white Mercedes-Benz and yellow taxis, driving along a multi-lane road. The final shot is a close-up of a young man in a gray hoodie, holding a smartphone to his ear, speaking animatedly with a focused expression, suggesting he is engaged in an important conversation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Kai-Fu Lee, a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, speaking at a tech event against a backdrop of AI and robotics company logos. His clear, authoritative voice discusses China's potential to popularize autonomous driving, acknowledging the US's technological lead but emphasizing China's larger market and government infrastructure support, such as building a new city the size of Chicago with autonomous driving capabilities and a new sensor-equipped highway for safer autonomous vehicles. A white Lincoln sedan with license plate '京Q·70VC8' is then seen driving on a highway, showcasing autonomous driving technology from front and side angles, as a male narrator states that Mercedes-Benz was among their early investors. The scene cuts to Cao Xudong, Founder & CEO of Momenta, a young man in a gray hoodie, speaking thoughtfully in an office setting, his voice expressing, "I feel very lucky and very inspiring and very exciting that we're living in this era." The video briefly returns to Kai-Fu Lee speaking before transitioning to a presentation titled 'How Momenta Works' in a large room with an attentive audience, including a man in a plaid shirt and a woman in a black top. Background music begins as a narrator explains that "Life in China is largely conducted on smartphones." The camera pans across the audience before shifting to a subway station where commuters are seen using smartphones. A close-up shows a hand interacting with a digital interface displaying icons for finance, shopping, and data, with a green box showing '800万' (8 million), as the narrator details how "A billion people use WeChat, the equivalent of Facebook, Messenger, and PayPal, and much more combined into just one super app. And there are many more." The final scene features a customer at a self-service kiosk in a 7FRESH Food Market, scanning a QR code for payment, with the screen displaying a total of 43.5 yuan and instructions for payment via WeChat or Alipay, while the narrator concludes, "China is the best place for AI implementation today."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a Man in Suit, wearing glasses, speaking thoughtfully and articulately in a dimly lit office, explaining that China possesses a vast amount of data, boasting significantly more users, 50 times more mobile payments, and 10 times more food deliveries than the US, all contributing to user behavior data. The scene then transitions to a bustling outdoor market where people, including a woman in a pink jacket and another person in a white coat, are actively using smartphones to scan QR codes for payments at fruit and vegetable stalls, visually illustrating the widespread mobile payment adoption he describes. The narrative shifts to urban mobility, showing a delivery rider in a yellow '美团外卖' uniform and another in a blue '达达' uniform on scooters, followed by a man scanning a QR code on a shared bicycle, and a crowded bike lane with numerous cyclists and pedestrians, reflecting the '300 times more shared bicycle rides' and delivery services mentioned by the speaker, with each bike submitting data to the cloud. The Man in Suit reappears, continuing his commentary, emphasizing that this translates to 10 times more data than the US, and that AI, fueled by data, works better with more of it, concluding that in the age of AI, where data is the new oil, China is the 'new Saudi Arabia' due to its data wealth. The video cuts to a nighttime urban setting where people interact with self-service kiosks, one displaying 'HANDMADE', before a wide-angle shot of a busy city street filled with pedestrians, many holding smartphones, captures the constant flow of people and technology, reinforcing the pervasive data generation. A second male voice briefly interjects, mentioning 'And access to all that data means the...', as the PBS and FRONTLINE logos appear.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern, open-plan office, where employees are diligently working at their desks, some with personal items like a Rubik’s cube and a potted plant. A narrator's voice explains that a deep learning algorithm can quickly predict behavior, such as the creditworthiness for a short-term loan. The scene then cuts to JIAO KE, identified by an on-screen 'FRONTLINE' logo and text as 'Founder & CEO, Smart Finance,' who is wearing a light blue shirt and glasses. He demonstrates the '用钱宝' (Yong Qian Bao) financial app on a white smartphone, adjusting a loan from ¥4000 to ¥3900, selecting a 3-month repayment term, and confirming it with a green button. JIAO KE, speaking with a clear, accented voice, explains that customers can choose their desired loan amount and duration, input their data, and borrow 'very quickly.' He smiles as he holds up the phone, showcasing the app. The narrator interjects, highlighting how quickly a loan can be obtained, to which JIAO KE adds, 'It has done,' and another voice exclaims 'Wow.' The camera pans across the office, showing other employees, including one with a monitor displaying code and another wearing sunglasses. The narrator continues, stating that the process takes an average of 8 seconds, during which JIAO KE confirms, 'It has passed two banks.' The narrator further elaborates that the algorithm assesses 5,000 personal features from all data, a point JIAO KE expands upon, noting that these features are related to delinquency, contrasting this with banks that 'only use few, maybe maybe 10 features' for risk management. JIAO KE is then seen in a direct interview, speaking to the camera from within the bright, tech-focused office environment with orange cubicle dividers and glass partitions.

The desk is decorated with small personal items, including a Rubik’s cube, a potted plant, and a nameplate with Chinese characters. The man smiles as he holds up the phone, showing the app interface. The camera then pans across the office, showing other employees working at their desks, one with a monitor displaying code and another wearing sunglasses. The scene transitions to a direct interview with the man, now identified by on-screen text as 'JIAO KE, Founder & CEO, Smart Finance,' speaking to the camera in the same office setting. The lighting is bright and professional, with orange cubicle dividers and glass partitions visible in the background, emphasizing a tech-focused, collaborative workspace.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a bustling urban street scene, capturing a dense crowd of pedestrians, many engrossed in their smartphones, reflecting a tech-saturated city life. A close-up of a smartphone screen displays a financial app interface with a green '确认借款' (Confirm Loan) button, as a male narrator explains how AI processes millions of transactions to uncover features like typing confidence during loan applications or even cell phone battery charge, which would be imperceptible to human loan officers. JIAO KE, Founder & CEO of Smart Finance, seen speaking directly to the camera in a modern office, elaborates on this, stating in a calm, professional tone that a phone's battery level is interestingly related to delinquency rates, with lower battery indicating higher risk. The scene transitions to the Smart Finance office, showing employees working at desks, including a woman in a plaid dress and a man in a gray shirt. The narrator then highlights China's dramatic technological evolution, exemplified by a facial recognition startup they invested in, identified as MakeV, founded in 2011 by three young graduates and now a world leader in AI identification. This is visually represented by a large digital display showing a facial recognition system scanning a woman’s face with a grid overlay and identification data, followed by a network of interconnected faces and data points, and finally, a blue-toned visualization of neural networks, symbolizing the underlying AI technology powering these smart finance and facial recognition systems. The overall atmosphere shifts from a chaotic, fast-paced city to a controlled, high-tech office environment, emphasizing the integration of AI and mobile technology in daily life and business operations.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a smartphone interface performing facial recognition, scanning a woman's face with a green circle indicating success, as Wu Wenhao, Vice President of Megvii, begins to speak, stating, "It's pretty fast, for example, on the mobile device... we have timed the facial recognition speed. It's actually less than 100 milliseconds. So that's very, very fast. So 0.1 second... that we can we'll be able to recognize you. Even on a mobile device." He is seen in an office with surveillance screens and the FRONTLINE logo. The scene then cuts to a live facial recognition system on a digital screen, showing a woman walking past a camera, her face scanned with a grid, displaying her name and age. The system identifies a man, showing his face, gender, age (27), and two captures, labeled '临时抓拍' (temporary capture) and '无登记' (unregistered), followed by a man with white hair identified as 69 years old. A narrator's voice explains, "The company claims the system is better than any human at identifying people in its database. And for those who aren't, it can describe them. Like our director. What he's wearing and a good guess at his age, missing it by only a few months." The video transitions to a modern, brightly lit restaurant where a woman in a white blouse approaches a self-service kiosk displaying a circular interface with a countdown (118s) and '28'. Wu Wenhao's voice returns, asserting, "We are the first one to really take facial recognition to commercial quality." The woman interacts with a staff member, receiving a small green item, as the narrator concludes, "That's why in Beijing today you can pay for your KFC with a smile." Another voice then adds, "You know, it's not so surprising we've seen the Chinese company..."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a large digital screen displaying a smartphone interface performing facial recognition, with a woman’s face being scanned and a green circle indicating successful identification. The scene transitions to Wu Wenhao, Vice President of Megvii, speaking directly to the camera in an office environment with multiple screens in the background, some showing surveillance footage. The FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower-left corner. The video cuts back to the digital screen, now showing a live facial recognition system in action: a woman walks past a camera, and her face is scanned with a grid overlay, displaying her name and age. The system then identifies a man, showing his face, gender, age (27), and number of captures (2), with the label '临时抓拍' (temporary capture) and '无登记' (unregistered). The system also analyzes a man with white hair, identifying him as 69 years old with similar data overlays. The scene shifts to a modern, brightly lit restaurant where a woman in a white blouse approaches a self-service kiosk. She looks at the screen, which displays a circular interface with a countdown timer (118s) and a number '28'. She then interacts with a staff member at the counter, who hands her a small green item, possibly a receipt or token, as she completes a transaction. The environment is clean and futuristic, emphasizing seamless, tech-driven customer service.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Paul Mozur, a man with a beard wearing a dark blue shirt, speaking in an office environment with computer monitors blurred in the background; identified by the FRONTLINE logo and as from The New York Times, he explains that China has been catching up to the US in technology for a long time, and if particular effort is paid to a specific sector, it's not surprising they would surpass the rest of the world, noting that facial recognition is one of the first places this has started to happen. The scene transitions to a multi-screen surveillance interface displaying real-time street footage with Chinese text overlays identifying people and vehicles like '小轿车' (small car) and '男' (male), indicating AI-driven object recognition. A male narrator states that this is a technology prized by the government, accompanied by a subtle electronic chime. The footage then shows a busy urban intersection with pedestrians and traffic, including a white car passing near a building labeled 'INGLORY PLAZA'. A large public screen displays a digital animation of a pedestrian crossing, followed by blurred faces of individuals labeled '历史发布 (闯红灯人员)' (historical release: jaywalkers), which the narrator explains is a program in Shenzhen to discourage jaywalking, where offenders are shamed in public and can be instantly fined with facial recognition. The video cuts to a close-up of a smartphone screen showing a credit score of 773 with Chinese text, including '信用良好' (good credit) and '芝麻信用' (Zhi Ma Credit), as the narrator warns that critics believe the government and private companies have been building a national database from dozens of experimental social credit programs. Next, Xiao Qiang, a research scientist from UC Berkeley, appears in a well-lit indoor setting, wearing a gray blazer over a black shirt, speaking with expressive hand gestures. He articulates that the government wants to integrate all these individual behaviors, or corporations' records, into some kind of a matrix to compute out a single number, or set of numbers, associated with an individual citizen. The scene shifts to an outdoor surveillance view of a sidewalk where people are tracked with colored bounding boxes, and a digital overlay appears showing a woman’s profile with details like '姓名: 范丹丹' (Name: Fan Dandan), '年龄: 36' (Age: 36), and a social credit score of 782, demonstrating this real-time biometric and credit data integration.

audio_updated_entities.Social Credit Display: Digital overlay on a surveillance feed showing a woman’s profile with personal data including '姓名: 范丹丹' (Name: Fan Dandan), '年龄: 36' (Age: 36), and a social credit score of 782, demonstrating real-time biometric and credit data integration as part of the social credit system that aims to assign a single number to citizens.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide-angle view of a brightly lit supermarket aisle, where a woman shopping in a dark sleeveless top is tracked by a 'Social Credit System' digital overlay. Her score increases from 782 to 785 as she picks up diapers, illustrating real-time biometric and behavioral monitoring. A male voice explains this system as an 'incentive or punishment system,' noting that a high social credit number can be rewarded with 'discounts on bus fares,' while a low number can lead to a 'travel ban.' The scene transitions to a bustling subway station with commuters, some carrying shopping bags, then to a crowded city street at dusk with pedestrians holding smartphones, a red traffic light, and a visible surveillance camera, emphasizing constant monitoring. A narrator's voice comments that some find the system popular for punishing bad behavior, while others see it as rewarding 'party loyalty' and 'silencing criticism.' The video cuts to a close-up interview with Xiao Qiang, a research scientist from UC Berkeley, speaking expressively in a well-lit indoor setting. He states that while no 'final system' is fully implemented, experiments already reveal the 'possibility of what this social credit system can do to individual,' describing it as 'very powerful, Orwellian-like' and 'extremely troublesome in terms of civil liberty.' The final shot shows a nighttime city street with a Citibank building, a red pedestrian signal, and multiple surveillance cameras mounted on a pole, as the narrator concludes that 'Every evening in Shanghai, ever-present cameras recording...' The environment shifts from bright indoor retail to dim, artificially lit urban streets, highlighting the pervasive nature of surveillance technology in daily life, underscored by the continuous discussion of the social credit system's implications.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a bustling nighttime scene on Shanghai's crowded riverside promenade, the Bund, where a dense crowd of people, including a woman holding a child and many using smartphones, moves and pauses to take photos under a lively, hazy atmosphere with warm streetlights. A calm, authoritative male narrator describes "the crowds as they surge down to the Bund," along the Huangpu River, recalling how "once the great trading houses of Europe came here to do business with the Middle Kingdom," before being "shut down by Mao's revolution." The camera pans across the crowd and the illuminated Pudong skyline, featuring the Oriental Pearl Tower and Shanghai Tower, their lights reflecting on the river, while a subtle, dramatic instrumental track plays. The narrator continues, stating that "now, in the age of AI, people come here to take in a spectacle that reflects China's remarkable progress and illuminates the great political paradox of capitalism taken root in the communist state." A police officer is briefly visible. The scene transitions to historic Bund buildings, lit in golden hues with Chinese flags, before cutting to an indoor interview. Here, Orville Schell, identified as being from the Asia Society and specializing in U.S.-China Relations, speaks directly to the camera, his thoughtful voice explaining, "People have called it market Leninism, authoritarian capitalism. We're watching a kind of a a petri dish in which an an experiment of um, you know, extraordinary uh uh importance to the world is being carried out. Uh whether you can combine these things and get something..." The FRONTLINE logo appears in the lower-left corner. The segment concludes with a return to the Shanghai skyline, the Oriental Pearl Tower glowing in red and blue, and a building displaying a red sign with Chinese characters, reinforcing the city’s vibrant, modern identity.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a bustling nighttime scene along a crowded riverside promenade, likely the Bund in Shanghai, where a dense crowd of people moves in various directions, some pausing to take photos. The atmosphere is lively and slightly hazy, with streetlights casting a warm glow. The camera pans across the crowd, capturing individuals of various ages, including a woman holding a small child and others using smartphones to record the view. In the background, the illuminated skyline of Pudong rises dramatically, featuring iconic structures like the Oriental Pearl Tower and the Shanghai Tower, their lights reflecting on the Huangpu River. The camera alternates between wide shots of the skyline and close-ups of the crowd, emphasizing the contrast between the modern urban landscape and the human activity in the foreground. A police officer in uniform is briefly visible, maintaining order amidst the throng. The scene transitions to a closer view of historic buildings on the Bund, their classical architecture lit in golden hues, with Chinese flags waving atop domes. The video then cuts to an indoor interview setting, where Orville Schell, identified by on-screen text as being from the Asia Society and specializing in U.S.-China Relations, speaks directly to the camera. He is an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark sweater over a collared shirt, seated in a softly lit room with blurred background decor. The FRONTLINE logo appears in the lower-left corner, indicating the documentary source. The segment concludes with a return to the Shanghai skyline, now with the Oriental Pearl Tower glowing in red and blue lights, and a large building displaying a red sign with Chinese characters, reinforcing the city’s vibrant, modern identity.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a sweeping panoramic view of Shanghai’s illuminated skyline at night, showcasing the iconic Bund buildings with their warm golden lights and modern skyscrapers, including the Oriental Pearl Tower glowing in the distance, as the Huangpu River reflects the city’s vibrant lights and boats glide across the water. The scene transitions to a close-up of Orville Schell, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark sweater over a collared shirt, speaking thoughtfully in an indoor interview setting with a calm, reflective tone, stating, "It's more powerful, it's coherent, it's durable in the world. Whether you can bring together a one-party state with an innovative sector, both economically and technologically innovative, uh, and that's something we thought could not coexist." The camera then returns to an aerial pan across the Shanghai skyline, emphasizing the contrast between historic architecture and futuristic towers, while a narrator explains, "As China reinvents itself, it has set its sights on leading the world in artificial intelligence by 2030. But that means taking on the world's most innovative AI culture." A title card reading 'TWO THE PROMISE' appears in white text over a dark, data-filled background, accompanied by a shift to more dramatic, tense background music. The scene shifts dramatically to a desert highway, where a blue semi-truck with 'EMBARK' branding is shown from an overhead drone perspective, driving alone on a straight road. The camera follows the truck from various angles, including a side view and a rear mirror reflection, before cutting to the empty interior of the cab, highlighting the autonomous nature of the vehicle. The narrator continues, "On an interstate in the US Southwest, artificial intelligence is at work solving the problem that's become emblematic of the new age: replacing a human driver," as the steering wheel and dashboard are visible, with the desert landscape stretching endlessly through the windshield, underscoring the theme of advanced technology in isolation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a modern, open-plan office with blue walls and exposed wooden beams, where the company's 24-year-old CEO, Alex Rodriguez, wearing a black t-shirt with a blue logo, walks through the workspace, passing desks, chairs, and a whiteboard before moving toward a communal area with a gray sofa and yellow ottoman, where several people are engaged in conversation, accompanied by background office chatter. The scene transitions to a close-up of Alex Rodriguez, now seated and speaking directly to the camera, stating, "The more things we build successfully, the less people ask questions about how old you are when you have working tracks," with a blue semi-truck visible in the background. The video then cuts to an aerial view of the blue semi-truck, labeled 'EMBARK' and 'FRIGIDAIRE', driving on a desert highway, as a calm male voiceover explains, "And this is what he's built. Commercial goods are being driven from California to Arizona on Interstate 10." An interior shot shows a driver with a beard and sunglasses, hands on the steering wheel, though the voiceover clarifies, "There is a driver in the cab, but he's not driving," as the truck’s autonomous system is shown in action with a digital overlay indicating 'Engaged' and a speed of 73 MPH, navigating the highway. The voiceover adds, "It's a path set by a CEO with an unusual CV." The scene shifts to a workshop where a young boy with glasses, identified as Henry, operates a small robot, as a female voice asks, "Are we ready, Henry?" and Henry explains, "The aim is to score these pucks into the scoring area," accompanied by sounds of robot movement and clanking. This is followed by a group of teenagers, including Henry, smiling and posing together. Alex Rodriguez then explains his background, stating, "So I, I did competitive robotics starting when I was 11, and I took it very, very seriously. To, to give you a sense, I won the Robotics World Championships for the first time when I was 13. Been to Worlds seven times between the age of..." His speech cuts off as the video concludes with a group of teenagers in red shirts celebrating on stage at the 'FTC 2009 World Championship Awards Ceremony,' receiving awards and shaking hands with officials, visually reinforcing Alex Rodriguez's earlier mention of his robotics achievements.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a robotics team, clad in black 'ATA 4334' t-shirts, posing confidently with their robot in an indoor competition arena, as Alex Rodrigues's voiceover recounts finding a team and working at a very high competitive level. The scene transitions to an outdoor test track where a modified golf cart, labeled 'TECHNASTAR' and equipped with a laptop and sensors, is being tested. A young man in a black 'VELOCITY THALMIC LABS' t-shirt sits in the driver’s seat, while another man in a blue and white checkered shirt stands beside the cart, interacting with a laptop. A narrator identifies this as a prototype, from which Alex Rodrigues built his multi-million dollar company. Alex explains his motivation to get back into robotics, feeling this was the most exciting technology, and recounts building the self-driving golf cart in his parents' garage during a summer off. Close-ups highlight the cart’s autonomous technology, showing the laptop, steering wheel, and interior, as it drives autonomously around the track, navigating curves bordered by blue and white barrels. The narrator adds that this golf cart garnered the attention of Silicon Valley and the first of several rounds of venture capital. The scene briefly shifts to a modern office kitchen with people casually dining, before transitioning to an interview with Alex Rodrigues, CEO of Embark, who, wearing a black t-shirt with a blue 'EMBARK' logo, is seated in a workshop-like office with the FRONTLINE logo displayed. The narrator states they formed a team and decided the business opportunity was in self-driving trucks, with Alex concluding that there's a significant human benefit if they can build a truck ten times safer than a human driver.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Alex Rodrigues, a man with short dark hair and blue eyes, wearing a black t-shirt with a blue 'EMBARK' logo, speaking directly to the camera in a workshop-like office. He articulately states that autonomous driving is the "only way" to achieve "zero highway deaths," emphasizing that over 90% of crashes involve a human driver and calling traffic fatalities the "single biggest tragedy." As he gestures subtly, conveying confidence, the scene transitions to an exterior shot of a blue semi-truck, branded with 'FRIGIDAIRE' and 'EMBARK', driving autonomously across a desert highway under a clear sky, passing over a bridge, accompanied by a subtle, inspiring instrumental background music. Inside the truck, a monitor displays a real-time 3D sensor visualization of the road ahead, showing colorful lines for detected objects and lane markings. The view shifts to the truck’s roof, revealing an array of cameras and sensors mounted on a horizontal bar, capturing the highway. A close-up of the truck’s front grille and wheels emphasizes its motion. Inside the cab, a driver’s hands rest on the steering wheel, confirming autonomous operation. A first-person view from the truck’s front shows vibrant purple and teal digital lane lines, a '74 MPH' speed indicator, and an 'Engaged' toggle, all confirming the active autonomous system. The video then returns to Alex Rodrigues, who continues speaking in the same workshop setting, maintaining eye contact. He explains that artificial intelligence and deep learning are key pieces that have made driverless vehicles possible, particularly the ability to "see and understand scenes," noting that this was "remarkably hard for computers until very, very recently."

He discusses the significant strides made in artificial intelligence, enabling machines to perform even the most basic visual tasks and understand the world through available sensors, all while positioned in a workshop-like office with blurred blue machinery in the background. The scene then transitions to an overhead view of a blue semi-truck, branded 'EMBARK', driving autonomously on a desert highway, accompanied by the distinct sound of a truck. An interior shot from the driver’s perspective further emphasizes the empty cab and the road ahead, highlighting the absence of a human operator. A narrator's voice then explains that this capability is due to algorithms developed by Yashua Benjio and a small group of scientists. The video then cuts to a man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red collared shirt, who is first seen writing technical diagrams and terms like 'constant' and 'tangent' on a whiteboard with a blue marker. He then turns to speak directly to the camera, gesturing expressively with his hands, explaining in an academic and explanatory tone that many aspects of the world cannot be explained with words, and that verbally communicable knowledge is merely the 'tip of the iceberg'. He elaborates that the solution involves computers acquiring knowledge by themselves from data and examples, much like children learn, not primarily from teachers but from... (the audio fades). The professional and educational atmosphere is maintained by soft, focused lighting and the consistent whiteboard background throughout his segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a retro-style arcade game, resembling Breakout, displayed on a black screen with a blue border, featuring a paddle, a bouncing ball, and a multi-colored brick wall. Classic arcade sound effects, including paddle hits and brick breaks, accompany the gameplay as a male narrator explains how AI programs learn by "interacting with the world and playing around." He introduces this as an early 2013 demonstration by DeepMind scientists, where a machine learning program was set on the Atari Breakout game, only given the goal to win. After 100 games, the program, accompanied by increasing game sounds, learned to hit the ball and break bricks. A black screen with white text 'AFTER 300 GAMES' appears, followed by a return to the game, now with a significantly higher score and more broken bricks, as the narrator states it could play better than a human. The background behind the game transitions to a dark, mathematically dense pattern of equations and code. Another black screen reads 'AFTER 500 GAMES', and the game resumes with even more advanced play, demonstrating a creative strategy of digging a tunnel on the side to break many bricks with one hit, which the narrator identifies as "deep learning." The scene then cuts to an interview with Yoshua Bengio, a man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, articulate voice. A lower-third graphic identifies him as 'YOSHUA BENGIO, University of Montreal' with the 'FRONTLINE' logo in the lower-left corner, against a dimly lit room with a whiteboard. Bengio discusses how this AI program, based on learning, has been "so successful in the last few years" and "completely changed" the field.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Yoshua Bengio, a man with gray hair and a beard, wearing a red collared shirt, speaking directly to the camera in an interview setting. His voice is heard stating that something "is now used in almost every sector of society." Behind him, a dimly lit room features a whiteboard with faint mathematical diagrams and text like 'constant' and 'tangent'. A lower-third graphic identifies him as 'YOSHUA BENGIO, University of Montreal' with the 'FRONTLINE' logo. The scene then cuts to a woman with curly dark hair and black-rimmed glasses, wearing a black leather jacket over a white blouse with a floral pattern, seated at a cluttered desk in a well-lit office. Her voice begins, explaining that "Even the best and brightest among us, we just don't have enough compute power inside of our heads." She is initially focused on a computer monitor adorned with stickers, including a wolf and a dog, while typing and occasionally glancing up. A narrator then identifies her as "Amy Webb, a professor at NYU and founder of the Future Today Institute." The camera captures Amy from various angles, including a side profile and a close-up of her face as she speaks animatedly, gesturing with her hands. She continues, asserting that "As AI progresses, the great promise is that these machines alongside of us are able to think and imagine and see things in ways that we never have before," suggesting they might offer "some kind of new, weird, seemingly implausible solution to climate change" or a "radically different approach to dealing with incurable cancers." She concludes that the "real practical and wonderful promise is that machines help us be more creative." The background reveals a framed certificate on the wall and a softly lit lamp, suggesting a professional yet personal workspace. The lighting remains consistent, with natural light filtering through a window, creating a calm and thoughtful atmosphere, as Amy’s expressions shift from concentration to engaged communication, indicating a dynamic and reflective dialogue.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of Massachusetts General Hospital, showcasing its modern glass facade and bustling entrance, as an ambulance drives through its emergency entrance, its siren wailing. A male narrator speaks of finding "terrific solutions" to "urgent problems." The scene transitions indoors to Dr. Connie Lehman, a woman in a white lab coat and black turtleneck, wearing glasses, who speaks directly to the camera, stating that early detection "is going to change the face of breast cancer," noting that "40,000 women in the US alone die from breast cancer every single year." Dr. Lehman, seen seated at a desk analyzing mammogram images displayed on dual monitors labeled 'RIGHT' and 'LEFT', emphasizes that "we've become so complacent about it," and that while energy is often put into chemotherapy for metastatic breast cancer, "when we find it early, we cure it" without the "ravages to the body" of late diagnosis. The camera zooms in on her focused expression as she examines the scans. In a dimly lit room, Dr. Lehman collaborates with a blonde colleague, pointing at a large screen displaying multiple breast imaging views, including 3D reconstructions. Dr. Lehman explains, "This shows the progression of a small, small spot from one year to the next and then to the diagnosis of the small cancer here." The two women discuss the images, with Dr. Lehman gesturing toward specific areas of concern. The video concludes with a close-up of the detailed mammogram images, highlighting dense tissue patterns and anatomical structures, as the male narrator begins to speak about "what happened when a woman who had..."

segments.integrated_caption: A male narrator opens the segment, discussing breast cancer diagnoses and the critical questions arising from them. Visually, Professor Regina Barzilai, a woman with long, light brown hair, wearing a dark top and patterned scarf, is seen in a dimly lit room surrounded by computer monitors and medical imaging equipment. She speaks with a distinct accent, conveying the profound anxiety of a diagnosis, asking, "am I going to survive? What's going to happen to my son?" and stating she began asking "other questions." The scene transitions to an outdoor shot of a modern, angular building with orange and white facades, likely a medical or research facility, with people walking across a plaza, as the PBS logo briefly appears. The male narrator then formally introduces Professor Regina Barzilai from MIT's Artificial Intelligence Lab, detailing her work using deep learning to teach computers to understand language, text, and data. The video cuts to Professor Barzilai working at a desk, typing on a laptop in an office, where she expresses, in her distinct accent, her surprise that "excellent physicians" at MGH couldn't answer her basic questions. A close-up follows of a hand holding a blurred Polaroid photo of a woman’s face, adding a personal layer, with the FRONTLINE logo appearing. The male narrator continues, explaining Barzilai's conviction that analyzing sufficient data from mammograms to diagnostic notes could enable computers to predict early stage conditions. The narrative returns to the dimly lit imaging room, where Professor Barzilai is now seated beside a woman in a white lab coat, who points to a large screen displaying detailed mammogram images. These teal and black-and-white scans show breast tissue with anatomical labels like 'Rmcc' and 'Lmlo' and measurements such as '173 µm', as the lab coat woman gestures toward specific areas, indicating a diagnostic review. The camera zooms in on the mammogram images, highlighting dense tissue patterns and potential abnormalities, before a new female voice begins, "If we fast forward from 2012..."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a large monitor displaying teal and black mammogram images, labeled with anatomical markers like 'LMLO' and 'Rmcc'. Constance Lehman, M.D., Chief of Breast Imaging at Mass. General Hospital, in a white lab coat, points to specific areas, her voice explaining the analysis of a 'spot' on a mammogram from '2013 to 2014,' questioning if 'more elegant computer applications' could have identified it earlier. The scene transitions to a dimly lit imaging workstation where Lehman continues her analysis, gesturing toward 3D reconstructions and detailed scans. The camera then cuts to an interview with Regina Barzilay from MIT, her long brown hair framing her face, speaking directly to the camera with a serious expression, blurred mammogram images in the background. With a distinct accent, Barzilay describes 'standard prediction problems in machine learning' as 'nothing special,' but expresses surprise that MIT's 'technologies,' even in their 'most simple form, doesn't penetrate the hospital.' A male narrator's voice then details how 'Regina and Carney began the slow process of getting access to thousands of mammograms and records from MGH's breast imaging program.' The setting shifts back to the professional, clinical imaging room, where Lehman, now wearing glasses and a black turtleneck under her lab coat, discusses images with a blonde colleague in a dark top who listens intently. Lehman's voice returns, explaining their 'first foray' was to gather data from 'all of the patients we had at MGH' who had undergone 'breast surgery for a certain type of high-risk lesion.' The FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower-left corner during the interview.

The scene transitions to a dimly lit imaging workstation with multiple monitors, where Lehman continues her analysis, gesturing toward the 3D reconstructions and detailed scans. The camera then cuts to an interview setting with Regina Barzilay from MIT, who speaks directly to the camera with a serious expression, her long brown hair framing her face, while blurred mammogram images appear in the background. The setting shifts back to the imaging room, where Lehman, now wearing glasses and a black turtleneck under her lab coat, discusses the images with a colleague, a blonde woman in a dark top, who listens intently. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Constance Lehman, M.D., Chief of Breast Imaging at Mass. General Hospital, wearing glasses, a black turtleneck, and a white lab coat, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit imaging room. Behind her, monitors display mammogram images in teal and black-and-white. She explains, "we found that most of them didn't really need the surgery, they didn't have cancer, but about 10% did have cancer." She continues, detailing how "With Regina's techniques and deep learning and machine learning, we were able to predict the women that truly needed the surgery and separate out those that really could avoid the unnecessary surgery." The scene transitions to Regina Barzilay, a woman with long brown hair, wearing a dark top with a patterned scarf, speaking expressively in an interview setting with blurred mammogram images in the background. She elaborates on machine capabilities, stating, "What machine can do, it can take hundreds of thousands of images where the outcome is known, and then, based on how, you know, pixels are distributed, what are the very unique patterns that correlate highly with future occurrence of the disease." Barzilay further explains that "instead of using human capacity to kind of recognize pattern, formalize pattern, which is inherently limited by our cognitive capacity and how much we can see and remember, we're providing machine with a lot of data and make it learn this prediction." The final shot shows Constance Lehman and a blonde colleague seated side-by-side, examining a 3D mammogram image on a large monitor, with Lehman pointing to specific areas, as Lehman briefly states, "So we are using techni..." before the audio cuts off. The overall environment is professional and focused, with soft lighting emphasizing the screens and the serious nature of the medical work.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Constance Lehman, M.D., Chief of Breast Imaging at Mass. General Hospital, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair, glasses, a black turtleneck, and a white lab coat, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit imaging room. In a clear, professional voice, she discusses how "technology" and "artificial intelligence machine and deep learning" can improve breast density assessment and predict cancer risk. Behind her, multiple monitors display mammogram images. The scene transitions to show her seated beside a blonde colleague, collaboratively reviewing mammogram images on a large screen. This is followed by a dynamic digital animation of mathematical equations and circuitry, culminating in a glowing blue digital brain composed of data points, set against a dramatic, futuristic synth score. A male voiceover declares, "In the age of AI, the algorithms are transporting us into a universe of vast potential and transforming almost every aspect of human endeavor and experience." This voice then introduces "Andrew McAfee," a research scientist at MIT and co-author of "The Second Machine Age." The video cuts to Andrew McAfee, a bald man with a short beard in a light blue shirt, standing in front of a projection screen displaying a 'Labor and Profit Share' line graph. He gestures as he articulately explains, "The great compliment that a songwriter gives another one is, gosh, I wish I'd written that one. The great compliment a geek gives another one is, wow, I wish I had drawn that graph. So, I wish I had drawn this graph." The camera zooms in on his face, then cuts to a close-up of a 'Social Development Scores' graph. The male voiceover concludes, stating, "The graph uses a formula to show human development and growth since 2000 BCE."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a line graph titled 'Social Development Scores, 2000 BCE - 2000 CE', displaying three colored lines (blue for 'West', green for 'East', and a red line) tracking development over time. The graph initially shows relatively flat lines, visually supporting Andrew McAfee's voiceover explaining that the 'state of human civilization is not very advanced' and 'not getting better very quickly at all' for 'thousands and thousands of years,' even through the rise of empires, democracy, and mathematical discoveries, as 'the numbers don't change very much.' The scene then cuts to McAfee, a bald man with a short beard in a light blue button-down shirt, seated in an empty lecture hall with tiered maroon seats, speaking expressively with hand gestures. The FRONTLINE logo and his MIT affiliation are visible. The video alternates back to the graph, which zooms in on the period after 1500 CE, showing the blue and green lines sharply rising. McAfee's voice describes this dramatic shift, stating that 'the numbers changed essentially in the blink of an eye,' going from 'really horizontal, un-changing, un-interesting to holy Toledo, crazy vertical.' He then poses the question of what caused this change, answering that it was the 'Industrial Revolution,' which fundamentally allowed humanity to 'overcame the limitations of our muscle power.' He concludes by noting that 'something equally interesting is happening right now,' as 'we are overcoming the limitations of our minds.' The lecture hall lighting is warm and focused on McAfee, while the graph is presented on a clean, neutral background with clear text.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Andrew McAfee, a bald man with a short beard wearing a light blue button-down shirt, seated in an empty lecture hall with tiered maroon seats in the background. He speaks expressively, using hand gestures, stating, "how can we leverage them and amplify them now? You have to be a huge pessimist not to find that profoundly good news." The scene transitions to Brad Smith, President of Microsoft, who appears in a modern office setting with large windows. Wearing a blue checkered suit jacket, he speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with both hands as he explains, "I really do think the world has entered a new era. Artificial intelligence holds so much promise, but it's going to reshape every aspect of the economy, so many aspects of our lives, because AI is a little bit like electricity. Everybody's going to use it. Every company is going to be incorporating AI, integrating it into what they do. Governments are going to be using it, non-profit organizations are going to be using it. It's going to create all kinds of benefits in ways large and small and challenges for us as well." A deep male narrator then interjects, "The challenges, the benefits." An aerial view of a desert highway shows a white semi-truck with 'EMBARK' branding on the trailer navigating a curve, as the narrator continues, "The autonomous truck represents both, as it maneuvers into the marketplace." The camera zooms in to show a worker in a yellow safety vest inspecting the truck’s side, followed by a close-up of the truck’s interior, revealing an empty driver’s seat and a dashboard with multiple screens and sensors. The scene shifts to a tech office environment, where a group of engineers in casual attire are intensely focused on computer monitors, discussing code and data. The narrator concludes, "The engineers are confident that in spite of questions about when this will happen, they can get it working safely sooner than most people realize," before an unidentified voice begins to say, "I think that you will see..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Alex Rodrigues, CEO of Embark, speaking clearly and confidently from a workshop-like office, explaining the imminent arrival of autonomous freight vehicles within "less than half a decade" and their expansion across more geographies and weather conditions. The FRONTLINE logo is visible in the lower-left. As he speaks, a distinct truck air brake hiss and a "ding" sound punctuate his words. A male narrator then adds context, stating that Embark already has a Fortune 500 client shipping appliances across the Southwest. Visually, a white semi-truck branded 'EMBARK' and 'FRIGIDAIRE' is shown driving past a warehouse, its engine rumbling as it enters a loading dock, viewed from inside the industrial space where an American flag hangs in the distance. Alex Rodrigues returns to the screen, continuing to detail the straightforward sales pitch: autonomous trucks can halve shipping costs and offer 24-hour operation, unlike human drivers limited to 11 hours by federal law. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the 'EMBARK' truck navigating a winding desert highway, emphasizing its driverless capability. The minute concludes with a static shot of a weathered, torn billboard against a pale sky, symbolizing obsolescence, accompanied by a gentle, melancholic acoustic guitar melody and a subtle synth pad.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a wide exterior shot of a truck stop or commercial area under overcast daylight, featuring multiple semi-trucks moving or parked, and a prominent 'TRUCK WASH' sign on a turquoise building, all set to gentle, melancholic acoustic guitar music. A male narrator introduces the segment, explaining that the idea of driverless trucks is often discussed in relation to AI. He then identifies the bearded man, seen in an interior close-up driving a car with focused hands on the steering wheel, as Steve Vaselli, a sociologist who drove a truck while researching his book, 'The Big Rig.' The scene transitions to Steve Vaselli in an indoor interview setting, seated at a table with clasped hands against a background of a window, decorative paper, and a wall-mounted device. He speaks directly to the camera, describing the decline of unionized trucking as one of the most remarkable stories in US labor history. He explains that after the industry was deregulated in 1980, truck drivers' earnings, which were once equivalent to over $100,000 in today's dollars, now stand at just over $40,000 a year for a typical driver. Brief cuts to a highway with heavy traffic, including blue and red semi-trucks under a clear sky, and an exterior shot of a white Volvo semi-truck with 'Eagle' branding driving past the truck wash, punctuate his interview. Steve Vaselli continues, his expression serious and engaged, linking the fear of automation to four decades of rising inequality, as the acoustic guitar music plays softly throughout.

The scene cuts to an interior close-up of a bearded man in a dark jacket and plaid shirt, driving a car with his hands on the steering wheel, looking focused ahead. The video then transitions to an indoor interview setting where the same man, now seated at a table with his hands clasped, speaks directly to the camera. The final shot returns to the interview, with the man continuing to speak, his expression serious and engaged.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male narrator discussing wages and the impact of automation on the trucking industry, noting that drivers are "first in line" for its effects. Concurrently, a dark blue Kia SUV drives down a frosty, rural dirt road, its engine sound audible, approaching a white house with a red roof and an American flag on the porch. The vehicle turns onto the driveway and parks. The narrator then introduces the research of "Sully" (Steve Vaselli) tracking truckers like Sean and Hope Cumby of Beaverton, Michigan. A man, Steve Vaselli, in a dark jacket and jeans, exits the car and is warmly greeted by a woman, Hope Cumby, with glasses and dark hair, wearing a gray top with cutout sleeves. They embrace, and Steve enters the house. Inside, their young son, Charlie, in a bright green hoodie, points to a framed family photo on the wooden wall, identifying "Daddy, me, Daddy, and Mommy" in a child's voice. The narrator clarifies that "Daddy" (Sean Cumby) is not present because his truck has broken down in Tennessee. The scene shifts to the kitchen, where Hope, described by the narrator as having driven a truck herself and knowing the business well, sits at a small table with Steve, both holding mugs. Hope speaks directly to the camera with an earnest, conversational voice, detailing the financial realities of trucking, explaining how they made "$150,000" in a year but paid "$100,000 in fuel," leaving "$50,000" before other expenses like oil. Steve listens attentively, occasionally sipping from his mug, as a decorated Christmas tree adds a festive, warm atmosphere to their intimate, domestic conversation during the cold season.

A man in a dark jacket and jeans exits the car and walks to the front door, where he is greeted warmly by a woman with glasses and dark hair, wearing a gray top with cutout sleeves. They embrace, and the man enters the house. Inside, a young boy in a bright green hoodie points to a framed family photo on the wooden wall, which shows the man, woman, and a baby. The scene shifts to the kitchen, where the couple sits at a small table, holding mugs, with a decorated Christmas tree visible in the background. The atmosphere is intimate and domestic, suggesting a personal, reflective moment within a family home during a cold season.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens in a warm, intimate indoor interview setting, where a woman with dark curly hair and glasses, wearing a gray top, sits at a wooden kitchen table with a bearded man. Her expressive voice dominates the initial segment, detailing significant financial challenges, including a "$13,000 motor blowout" and the struggle to manage "$300 a month" in expenses, ultimately making "about $22,000" by year-end. She speaks with emotion, reflecting on the difficulties of being "off work for two weeks." The scene then shifts dramatically to an industrial truck repair shop at night, where a large white semi-truck named 'Betsy Mae,' operated by 'Mid Seven Transportation,' is seen both outside and elevated on a red hydraulic lift inside the garage. A male narrator's voice explains that "Sean," a man in a black hoodie and a blue-and-red cap, is "sidelined" in Tennessee, waiting for a new part, with the garage owner allowing him to stay in the truck to save money. The sound of a heavy door closing punctuates the transition. Sean is then seen climbing into the truck's cab, adjusting a black communication headset with a glowing blue light, and his voice is heard engaging in a personal conversation, saying "Hi baby" and "Chunky butt" to a distant female voice. Finally, Sean stands beside his truck, speaking directly to the camera, his voice filled with passion as he explains, "Believe it or not, I do it because I love it," revealing he's a "third generation driver" and recalling his granddaddy's wisdom.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with an intimate indoor interview scene featuring a bearded man in a blue checkered shirt and a woman with dark curly hair, glasses, and a gray top with shoulder cutouts, seated at a wooden kitchen table. The setting is warm and domestic, with a glass of water on the table, a decorative paper arrangement, and a Christmas tree visible in the background, suggesting a cozy, personal environment. The camera alternates between close-ups of the two individuals as they speak, capturing subtle gestures and expressions.
